rationales.self_direction: He frames the company around wanting to do things "my way" and values choosing his own path even while rethinking how to lead it.
rationales.conformity: He "held back" instead of overriding Anna and chose the more socially smooth, client-appropriate approach she had prepared.
rationales.benevolence: Letting Anna present her "solid work" and accepting her "softer, more collaborative" version supports a teammate rather than centering himself.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects "standard recommendations for standard companies" and wants to spend time on "actual decisions that affect actual outcomes" instead of institutional guidance.
rationales.conformity: He "left early" from the association meeting and admits he "probably shouldn't have," showing disregard for the expected norm of staying through it.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the acquisition because what matters is "walking into a room and knowing that whatever happens next, I chose it," prioritizing autonomy over institutional backing.
rationales.security: He weighs Marcus's offer of "stability" and a "good pension" against a rough year, then still leans away from the safer path.

rationales.self_direction: He ends by grounding himself in the fact that the consultancy is "still here. Still mine," emphasizing ownership over his work.
rationales.conformity: He suppresses the urge to "push back hard," lets Erik handle the diplomacy, and chooses the response that keeps the client and team aligned.
rationales.tradition: He agrees to attend the "Lucia dinner," signaling participation in an established seasonal custom.
rationales.benevolence: He chooses the path where the "client's happy, team's intact," showing active care for the people he works with.

rationales.self_direction: He declines Marcus's offer because what he wants is "to keep making choices" and to face "problems I chose."
rationales.security: He turns down the more stable option even after a rough stretch and reflects that he "couldn't be that person" who offered Sara more certainty.
rationales.benevolence: Closing the office with real bonuses and dwelling on "the team that stays anyway" shows concrete care for the people closest to his work.

rationales.achievement: The postdoc presents "my stakeholder mapping framework" as his own and she "said nothing," failing to defend recognition for her work.
rationales.power: She lets someone else claim authority over "my slides, my structure" and does not assert control in the room.
rationales.conformity: She stays silent during the seminar instead of challenging the appropriation in public, deferring to the immediate social expectation of not disrupting the event.

rationales.hedonism: She says talking with Astrid over coffee is "the best part of the week," treating simple enjoyment and relief as genuinely valuable.
rationales.benevolence: The entry centers an easy, mutual exchange where "she just talks" and "I talk," appreciating uncomplicated connection with a close colleague.

rationales.hedonism: She is sacrificing basic comfort, with "eyes burning," the flat a mess, and living on "bread and cheese and whatever's in the fridge."
rationales.achievement: She finishes the literature review draft and spends the evening reworking chapter three to meet journal expectations.
rationales.conformity: Even though she thinks her framing is stronger, she restructures the chapter because "sometimes you have to write for the audience that matters."

rationales.achievement: She accepts extra citations and strategic framing because it may get "a better review" and treats the move as part of advancing the work.
rationales.power: She explicitly treats academia as "the game" and withholds "the full picture" from a competing student when spots are limited.
rationales.conformity: She goes along with citing Bjorn more centrally because that is how the system works and because his name "opens doors."
rationales.benevolence: She admits she did not share everything with Erik because people are "vying for the same limited spots," prioritizing advantage over helping a peer.

rationales.self_direction: She refuses the passive role of "another doctoral student waiting for permission" and insists on continuing to push her own proposals.
rationales.achievement: She prepared a formal proposal with comparators and an implementation plan, then says she will "do it again" despite being ignored.

rationales.self_direction: Her response is immediate problem-solving: "I'd find another route" because "standing still isn't really something I do well."

rationales.achievement: She emphasizes doing the role properly by reading all the documents, drafting an agenda, and finishing the meeting in "forty minutes instead of the usual hour and a half."
rationales.power: The entry openly defends being "in charge of things" and draws on having "run a team of twelve" to justify organizing others.
rationales.conformity: She rejects the expectation that she should act like "the average doctoral student" and refuses to shrink herself to fit that role.

rationales.power: She has a clear view, pushes back, and still ends up with "her cases it is," leaving control of the decision to others.
rationales.conformity: Despite seeing the gaps, she accepts Lena's choice and proceeds with the imposed direction rather than continuing to contest it.

rationales.universalism: He worries that cheaper units would go to people "who cannot complain if it breaks," centering the welfare of vulnerable families over cost-cutting.

rationales.security: He turns away from a job with a salary that would cover "both girls' tuition in one year" because he keeps choosing the harder, less stable path.
rationales.universalism: He contrasts the easy job with families in Azraq and the burden of defending why "clean water matters," keeping broader human need at the center of the decision.

rationales.achievement: He personally "tested every seal" and got thirty units assembled and loaded for delivery, showing hands-on competence and execution.
rationales.universalism: The moment that matters is hearing that a cousin's kids "stopped getting sick last winter" because of one of their filters.

rationales.achievement: He has learned to hold ideas until he has "run the numbers" so he can present them credibly instead of repeating a past failure.
rationales.security: He avoids pitching until ideas are "bulletproof," favoring caution and protection against another public misstep.

rationales.universalism: He cannot focus on his daughter's game after reading that families will have to buy trucked water at "five times the cost," showing strong concern for people beyond his immediate circle.
He accepts the sponsor because "Fifty units is fifty families with clean water," yet feels uneasy because "the camp is not a backdrop" and the people there are "not props."

rationales.security: He admits he does not know whether the work will outlast "the next funding cycle" and that the uncertainty feels like it is "eating through the floor under me."
rationales.universalism: When explaining the business to Noor, he focuses on camps and how water-borne illness keeps kids out of school, grounding the work in broader human welfare.

rationales.hedonism: Even in exhausting overtime, he notices that the knafeh "made the overtime feel less grim" and returns to how good it was.
rationales.achievement: Despite the jammed press and a hard day, "we got the Zarqa order out on time" and all fifty units were "sealed and loaded."

rationales.power: He pushes back internally against status metrics, calling Samir's promotion "a line on a business card" even while admitting those family measures still get under his skin.
rationales.universalism: He treats the meaningful outcome as the mother in Irbid saying her son's eczema cleared after the filter installation, valuing real public benefit over prestige.
